title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the subway. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Iron Worm: Life Beneath the Streets
content.text: By the mid-19th century, London was the largest city in the world, but its streets were a scene of absolute chaos. Horse-drawn omnibuses, carts, and thousands of pedestrians fought for space in narrow alleys that had never been designed for such a population. The solution proposed by Charles Pearson was as radical as it was terrifying to the public: a railway that ran beneath the ground. ||(41)|| [41]Despite these fears, the Metropolitan Railway opened in 1863[/41]. It used steam locomotives that burned coke to minimize smoke, but the experience was still a sulfurous, choking ordeal for passengers. Nevertheless, it was an instant success, proving that people were willing to endure a bit of soot if it meant bypassing the gridlock above. ||(42)|| [42]This technological shift toward electricity allowed for the construction of 'The Tube.' Unlike the early shallow tunnels, these new lines were bored deep into the London clay using a circular shield[/42]. This meant that the railway could pass directly under buildings without disturbing their foundations, leading to the spiderweb of lines we know today. ||(43)|| [43]Across the Atlantic, New York City was watching London’s experiment with great interest[/43]. The city’s first attempt at a subway was a secret pneumatic tube built by Alfred Ely Beach in 1870. It was a single-block demonstration where a plush car was literally blown through a tunnel by a massive fan. ||(44)|| [44]The real New York Subway finally opened in 1904, operated by the Interborough Rapid Transit Company (IRT)[/44]. It was an engineering marvel that featured four tracks, allowing for express trains to fly past local stations. This innovation turned the subway from a mere transport link into a high-speed artery that fueled the city's northward expansion. ||(45)|| [45]However, the subway was more than just a way to get to work[/45]. During the 20th century, it became a cultural canvas. In the 1970s and 80s, New York’s trains were covered in vibrant, controversial graffiti, a visual manifestation of a city in the midst of social and economic upheaval. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the world’s subways are becoming 'smart.' Automated trains, platform screen doors, and contactless payments are the new standard[/46]. Yet, as we glide through these high-tech tunnels, we are still following the paths laid down by the Victorian pioneers who first dared to imagine a life beneath the pavement.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] critics of the plan [/41] were vocal. They warned that the tunnels would collapse under the weight of the city, or that the 'foul air' underground would lead to mass asphyxiation. Some religious leaders even argued that venturing into the earth was a literal intrusion into the kingdom of the devil.
paragraphs.G: This [42] smoky era of steam [/42] finally ended in 1890 with the opening of the City and South London Railway, the world’s first major deep-level electric underground. It was a cleaner, quieter, and much more efficient way to move millions of commuters through the dark.
paragraphs.C: This [43] method of deep-level tunneling [/43] was quickly adopted by other major cities. Paris followed with its 'Métro' in 1900, which featured elegant Art Nouveau entrances that made the subway feel like a sophisticated extension of the city's boulevards rather than a dark basement.
paragraphs.E: However, [44] this 'wind-powered' dream [/44] was short-lived. Due to political opposition and a lack of funding, the project was abandoned, and it would be another thirty years before the city finally broke ground on a permanent, steam-free underground system.
paragraphs.B: The [45] social impact of the underground [/45] was equally profound. It broke down class barriers as bankers sat next to laborers, and it allowed the working class to live in healthier 'suburbs' while still having access to the jobs in the city center.
paragraphs.D: This [46] artistic era has largely passed [/46], replaced by sleek, stainless steel cars and digital advertising. While the trains are cleaner now, some miss the raw energy of the 'rolling galleries' that once defined the gritty character of the metropolitan experience.
paragraphs.F: Many [47] modern subways [/47] now feature deep-level bunkers designed to double as air-raid shelters. This dual-purpose architecture was proven vital during the Blitz in London, where thousands of families spent their nights on the platforms to escape the bombing.

questions.explanation: A describes the 'critics of the plan' and their fears, which provides the contrast to the 'solution proposed by Charles Pearson' mentioned before the gap.
G refers back to the 'smoky era of steam' and introduces the electric underground, which explains the 'technological shift' mentioned after the gap.
C follows the description of 'deep-level tunneling' in London and explains how the method was adopted in Paris and other cities.
E refers to the 'wind-powered' pneumatic tube mentioned before the gap and explains why it was eventually abandoned.
B explains the 'social impact' of the subway (breaking class barriers), providing context for its role as a 'high-speed artery' described in the text.
D refers back to the 'artistic era' of graffiti and contrasts it with the modern 'sleek, stainless steel' cars mentioned in the final paragraph.
